stem:
A 4-week-old infant presents to your emergency department with a 5-day history of progressive coughing fits. His mother describes bouts in which 'he goes red, then blue around the lips, then takes a deep noisy breath in and is exhausted'. He has fed poorly during these bouts. His sister had a cough 3 weeks ago and was told she had a viral chest infection. His mother did not receive the antenatal pertussis booster.

On examination he is afebrile and settled between paroxysms. There are bilateral subconjunctival haemorrhages and an audible inspiratory whoop is observed after one cough cluster in the department. The chest is clear on auscultation.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168, BP 78/46 mmHg, RR 56 (paroxysmal bursts to 80), SpO2 nadir 84% during paroxysm and 96% at rest, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Subconjunctival haemorrhages bilaterally, inspiratory whoop, chest clear to auscultation, fontanelle soft, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Total white cell count 32 x10^9/L (5.0-19.5), lymphocyte 22 x10^9/L (3.0-9.5), CRP 6 mg/L (<5), chest X-ray clear, nasopharyngeal swab PCR pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure the family, give paracetamol for comfort, and discharge home with cough safety-net advice
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with paroxysmal cough, whoop, and desaturations require admission for monitored apnoea risk; discharge underestimates progression and contact-tracing duty. (Source: CDNA SoNG Pertussis)
